001.student_question: What does "interconnection structure" actually mean in plain English?
001.source_chunks:
1. Interconnection Structures:
2. • Bus structures: Single bus, Multiple bus
3. • Instruction set Architecture: CISC, RISC
4. • Instruction and instruction sequencing:
5. • Register transfer notation, Instruction types.
6. Interconnection structures
7. • Computers has three main or basic modules: Processor, Memory and I/O
8. o The three basic modules must communicate with each other, therefore there must be paths for connecting these modules for them to communicate with each other.
9. The collection of paths connecting the various modules is called the interconnection structure. The design of this structure will depend on the exchanges that must be made among modules.
10. Structure (The Internal structure of the Computer)
11. A traditional computer employed a single processor and a microprogrammed control unit. simple single-processor computer, as shown in Figure 1.1 provides a hierarchical view of the internal structure of a traditional single-processor computer. There are four main structural components:
12. ■ Central processing unit (CPU): Controls the operation of the computer and performs its data processing functions; often simply referred to as processor.
13. ■ Main memory: Stores data.
14. ■ I/O: Moves data between the computer and its external environment.
15. ■ System interconnection: Some mechanism that provides communication among CPU, main memory, and I/O. A common example of system interconnection is by means of a system bus, consisting of a number of conducting wires to which all the other components attach. for
16. There may be one or more of each of the aforementioned components. Traditionally, there has been just a single processor, however, In recent years, there has been increasing use of multiple processors in a single computer.
17. The most interesting and in some ways the most complex component is the CPU. Its major structural components are as follows:
18. ■ Control unit: Controls the operation of the CPU and hence the computer.
19. ■ Arithmetic and logic unit (ALU): Performs the computer’s data processing functions.
20. ■ Registers: Provides storage internal to the CPU.
21. ■ CPU interconnection: Some mechanism that provides for communication among the control unit, ALU, and registers.
22. Complexity is added by the use of parallel and pipelined organizational techniques.
23. Finally, there are several approaches to the implementation of the control unit; one common approach is a microprogrammed implementation. In essence, a microprogrammed control unit operates by executing microinstructions that define the functionality of the control unit. With this approach, the structure of the control unit can be depicted, as in Figure 1.1.
001.qa_output:
An interconnection structure refers to the pathways that connect the main modules of a computer (CPU, memory, and I/O) so they can communicate.

Think of it like the road system in a city connecting different buildings (modules). The design of these pathways (interconnection structure) depends on how the modules need to exchange information.

002.student_question: If an I/O module is connected to three different things outside the computer, how does it know which one is talking to it?
002.source_chunks:
1. From an internal (to the computer system) point of view, I/O is functionally similar to memory.
2. There are two operations; read and write. Further, an I/O module may control more than one external device.
3. We can refer to each of the interfaces to an external device as a port and give each a unique address (e.g., 0, 1, M-1). In addition, there are external data paths for input and output of data with an external device.
4. Finally, an I/O module may be able to send interrupt signals to the processor.
5. The processor reads in instructions and data,
6. Writes out data after processing, and uses control signals to control the overall operation of the system.
7. It also receives interrupt signals.
8. The interconnection structure must support the following types of transfers:
9. ■ Memory to processor: The processor reads an instruction or a unit of data from memory.
10. ■ Processor to memory: The processor writes a unit of data to memory.
11. ■ I/O to processor: The processor reads data from an I/O device via an I/O module.
12. ■ Processor to I/O: The processor sends data to the I/O device.
13. ■ I/O to or from memory: For these two cases, an I/O module is allowed to exchange data directly with memory, without going through the processor, using direct memory access(DMA).
14. Figure 1 below shows the Types of exchanges that are needed by indicating the major forms of input and output for each module type.
15. Figure 1: Types of exchanges that are needed by indicating the major forms of input and output for each module type.
002.qa_output: An I/O module distinguishes between external devices by assigning a unique address, referred to as a port, to each interface connected to a device. This allows the module to direct communication to the correct device.

003.source_chunks:

2. There are two operations; read and write. Further, an I/O module may control more than one external device.

7. It also receives interrupt signals.
8. The interconnection structure must support the following types of transfers:
9. ■ Memory to processor: The processor reads an instruction or a unit of data from memory.
10. ■ Processor to memory: The processor writes a unit of data to memory.
11. ■ I/O to processor: The processor reads data from an I/O device via an I/O module.
12. ■ Processor to I/O: The processor sends data to the I/O device.
13. ■ I/O to or from memory: For these two cases, an I/O module is allowed to exchange data directly with memory, without going through the processor, using direct memory access(DMA).

003.qa_output: From an internal system perspective, I/O modules and memory are functionally similar because both utilize read and write operations. Additionally, I/O modules can send interrupt signals to the processor, a capability also associated with memory interactions. I/O modules can also exchange data directly with memory via Direct Memory Access (DMA), bypassing the processor.

004.student_question: What is DMA, and why is it a big deal that I/O can talk directly to memory without bothering the CPU?
004.source_chunks:
1. From an internal (to the computer system) point of view, I/O is functionally similar to memory.
2. There are two operations; read and write. Further, an I/O module may control more than one external device.
3. We can refer to each of the interfaces to an external device as a port and give each a unique address (e.g., 0, 1, M-1). In addition, there are external data paths for input and output of data with an external device.
4. Finally, an I/O module may be able to send interrupt signals to the processor.
5. The processor reads in instructions and data,
6. Writes out data after processing, and uses control signals to control the overall operation of the system.
7. It also receives interrupt signals.
8. The interconnection structure must support the following types of transfers:
9. ■ Memory to processor: The processor reads an instruction or a unit of data from memory.
10. ■ Processor to memory: The processor writes a unit of data to memory.
11. ■ I/O to processor: The processor reads data from an I/O device via an I/O module.
12. ■ Processor to I/O: The processor sends data to the I/O device.
13. ■ I/O to or from memory: For these two cases, an I/O module is allowed to exchange data directly with memory, without going through the processor, using direct memory access(DMA).
14. Figure 1 below shows the Types of exchanges that are needed by indicating the major forms of input and output for each module type.
15. Figure 1: Types of exchanges that are needed by indicating the major forms of input and output for each module type.
004.qa_output:
Direct Memory Access (DMA) is a mechanism that allows an I/O module to exchange data directly with main memory without involving the processor.

Significance:
*   **Frees the CPU**: By bypassing the processor, DMA allows the CPU to focus on executing instructions rather than managing data transfers.
*   **Improves efficiency**: This direct data exchange between I/O and memory reduces the overhead and latency associated with CPU intervention, leading to faster overall system performance.

005.student_question: Why does "Instructions" only go into the CPU? Where do they come from?
005.source_chunks:
1. From an internal (to the computer system) point of view, I/O is functionally similar to memory.
2. There are two operations; read and write. Further, an I/O module may control more than one external device.
3. We can refer to each of the interfaces to an external device as a port and give each a unique address (e.g., 0, 1, M-1). In addition, there are external data paths for input and output of data with an external device.
4. Finally, an I/O module may be able to send interrupt signals to the processor.
5. The processor reads in instructions and data,
6. Writes out data after processing, and uses control signals to control the overall operation of the system.
7. It also receives interrupt signals.
8. The interconnection structure must support the following types of transfers:
9. ■ Memory to processor: The processor reads an instruction or a unit of data from memory.
10. ■ Processor to memory: The processor writes a unit of data to memory.
11. ■ I/O to processor: The processor reads data from an I/O device via an I/O module.
12. ■ Processor to I/O: The processor sends data to the I/O device.
13. ■ I/O to or from memory: For these two cases, an I/O module is allowed to exchange data directly with memory, without going through the processor, using direct memory access(DMA).
14. Figure 1 below shows the Types of exchanges that are needed by indicating the major forms of input and output for each module type.
15. Figure 1: Types of exchanges that are needed by indicating the major forms of input and output for each module type.
16. The structure of a single core, which occupies a portion of the processor chip.
17. In general terms, the functional elements of a core are:
18. ■ Instruction logic: This includes the tasks involved in fetching instructions, and decoding each instruction to determine the instruction operation and the memory locations of any operands.
19. ■ Arithmetic and logic unit (ALU): Performs the operation specified by an instruction.
20. ■ Load/store logic: Manages the transfer of data to and from main memory via cache.
21. The core also contains an L1 cache, split between an instruction cache (I-cache) that is used for the transfer of instructions to and from main memory, and an L1 data cache, for the transfer of operands and results. Typically, today’s processor chips also include an L2 cache as part of the core.
22. In many cases, this cache is also split between instruction and data caches, although a combined, single L2 cache is also used.
23. Keep in mind that this representation of the layout of the core is only intended to give a general idea of internal core structure. In a given product, the functional
24. elements may not be laid out as the three distinct elements shown in Figure 1.2, especially if some or all of these functions are implemented as part of a microprommed control unit. Figure 1.3 is a photograph of the motherboard for a computer built around two Intel Quad-Core Xeon processor chips.
25. The most important in addition to the processor sockets:
26. ■ PCI-Express slots for a high-end display adapter and for additional peripherals .
27. ■ Ethernet controller and Ethernet ports for network connections.
28. ■ USB sockets for peripheral devices.
29. ■ Serial ATA (SATA) sockets for connection to disk memory Interfaces for DDR (double data rate) main memory chips
30. ■ Intel 3420 chipset is an I/O controller for direct memory access operations between peripheral devices and main memory
31. Read on the internal structure of a processor chip.
32. 1.3 Von Neumann architecture
33. In the 1940s, a mathematician called John Von Neumann described the basic arrangement (or architecture) of a computer.
34. Most computers today follow the concept that he described although there are other types of architecture.
35. When we talk about the Von Neumann architecture, we are actually talking about the relationship between the hardware that makes up a Von Neumann-based computer.
36. A Von Neumann-based computer is a computer that:
37. ▪ Uses a single processor.
38. ▪ Uses one memory ( and single path) for both instructions and data. A von Neumann computer cannot distinguish between data and instructions in a memory location! It ‘knows’ only because of the location of a particular bit pattern in RAM.
39. ▪ Executes programs by doing one instruction after the next in a serial manner using a fetch-decode-execute cycle.
40. A model of a Von Neumann computer system.
41. INSTRUCTION SEQUENCING
42. Four types of operations
43. 1. Data transfer between memory and processor registers.
44. 2. Arithmetic & logic operations on data
45. 3. Program sequencing & control
46. 4. I/O transfers.
47. 1)Register transfer notations (RTN)
48. • Right hand side of RTN-denotes a value.
49. • Left hand side of RTN-name of a location.
50. • Adding contents of R1, R2 & place sum in R3.
51. 3)Basic instruction types-(4 types)
52. • Three address instructions–
53. • Two address instructions-
54. Equivalent to : B <–[A] + [B]
55. • One address instruction –
56. Add contents of A to accumulator & store sum back to accumulator.
57. • Zero address instructions
58. Instruction store operands in a structure called push down stack.
59. Instruction execution & straight line sequencing
60. • The processor control circuits use information in PC to fetch & execute instructions one at a time in order of increasing address. This is called straight line sequencing.
61. • Executing an instruction-2 phase procedures.
62. • 1st phase–“instruction fetch”-instruction is fetched from memory location whose address is in PC. This instruction is placed in instruction register in processor
63. • 2nd phase-“instruction execute”-instruction in IR is examined to determine which operation to be performed.
64. To be continued
005.qa_output:
Instructions originate in memory. The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is designed to process instructions due to its dedicated logic for fetching, decoding, and executing them. This process is part of the Von Neumann architecture's fetch-decode-execute cycle, where the CPU retrieves instructions from memory using the Program Counter (PC) and stores them in the Instruction Register (IR) for processing.

Key terms:
- Memory: Storage for instructions and data.
- CPU: The processor that executes instructions.
- Program Counter (PC): A register holding the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
- Instruction Register (IR): A register holding the current instruction being executed.
- Fetch-decode-execute cycle: The fundamental process by which a CPU processes instructions.

006.student_question: What’s the main argument between CISC and RISC? Is it just complex vs. simple?
006.source_chunks:
1. The “best” way to design a CPU has been a subject of debate:
2. should the low-level commands be longer and powerful, using less individual instructions to perform a complex task (CISC), or should the commands be shorter and simpler, requiring more individual instructions to perform a complex task (RISC), but allowing less cycles per instruction and more efficient code?
3. ▪ CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) and RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) are two forms of CPU design.
4. ▪ CISC uses a large set of complex machine language instructions, while RISC uses a reduced set of simpler instructions.
5. RISC and CISC is another axis along which we can organize computer architectures ad their instructions and how they are executed.
6. Which of the design method of CISC and RISC is better in designing computer?
7. There is no “correct” answer: both approaches have pros and cons. ×86 CPUs (among many others) are CISC; ARM (used in many cell phones and PDAs), PowerPC, Sparc, and others are RISC.
8. • RISC: Reduce the cycles per instruction at the cost of the number of instructions per program.
9. • CISC: The CISC approach attempts to minimize the number of instructions per
10. • program but at the cost of an increase in the number of cycles per
11. Earlier when programming was done using assembly language, a need was felt to make instruction do more tasks because programming in assembly was tedious and error- prone due to which CISC architecture evolved but with the uprise of high-level language dependency on assembly reduced RISC architecture prevailed.
006.qa_output: The core debate between CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) and RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) concerns the design of a CPU's instruction set. CISC uses a large set of complex machine language instructions, aiming to minimize the number of instructions per program. RISC uses a reduced set of simpler instructions, aiming to reduce the cycles per instruction. The argument is not simply about complex versus simple, but a trade-off between the number of instructions needed to complete a task and the processing time (cycles) for each instruction. Historically, CISC emerged to simplify assembly programming, while RISC gained prominence with the rise of high-level languages. There is no single "correct" answer, as both approaches have advantages and disadvantages.

007.student_question: Why did people stop liking CISC as much when high-level programming languages became popular?
007.qa_output:
This response is not sourced from your study material.

High-level programming languages (HLLs) abstract away hardware details, making code more portable and easier to write. CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) architectures were designed when assembly language programming was prevalent, aiming to map complex HLL operations directly to single, intricate machine instructions. This reduced the number of instructions needed per program but often required many clock cycles per instruction.

When HLLs became dominant, the advantage of CISC's complex instructions diminished because compilers could generate efficient code using simpler instructions. RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) architectures, with their simpler, fixed-length instructions that execute in a single clock cycle, became more appealing. Compilers could optimize sequences of RISC instructions to achieve high performance, often outperforming CISC processors that relied on complex, multi-cycle instructions. The focus shifted from "instructions per program" to "cycles per instruction" for better overall efficiency.

Key points:
*   **High-level languages (HLLs):** Abstract machine details, promoting portability and ease of development.
*   **CISC:** Designed for assembly; complex instructions map to HLL operations, but are slow.
*   **RISC:** Simpler instructions, execute faster (fewer cycles per instruction); compilers optimize sequences.
*   **Shift:** HLLs made RISC's compiler-friendly approach more effective than CISC's direct hardware mapping.

008.student_question: How can a RISC instruction finish in just one clock cycle if the whole program ends up being way longer?
008.source_chunks:
1. The “best” way to design a CPU has been a subject of debate:
2. should the low-level commands be longer and powerful, using less individual instructions to perform a complex task (CISC), or should the commands be shorter and simpler, requiring more individual instructions to perform a complex task (RISC), but allowing less cycles per instruction and more efficient code?
3. ▪ CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) and RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) are two forms of CPU design.
4. ▪ CISC uses a large set of complex machine language instructions, while RISC uses a reduced set of simpler instructions.
5. RISC and CISC is another axis along which we can organize computer architectures ad their instructions and how they are executed.
6. Characteristic of RISC –
7. 1. Simpler instruction, hence simple instruction decoding.
8. 2. Instruction comes undersize of one word.
9. 3. Instruction takes a single clock cycle to get executed.
10. 4. More general-purpose registers.
11. 5. Simple Addressing Modes.
12. 6. Fewer Data types.
13. 7. A pipeline can be achieved.
14. Characteristic of CISC:
15. 1. Complex instruction, hence complex instruction decoding.
16. 2. Instructions are larger than one-word size.
17. 3. Instruction may take more than a single clock cycle to get executed.
18. 4. Less number of general-purpose registers as operations get performed in memory itself.
19. 5. Complex Addressing Modes.
20. 6. More Data types.
21. Example – Suppose we have to add two 8-bit numbers:
22. • CISC approach: There will be a single command or instruction for this like ADD which will perform the task.
23. • RISC approach: Here programmer will write the first load command to load data in registers then it will use a suitable operator and then it will store the result in the desired location.
24. So, add operation is divided into parts i.e. load, operate, store due to which RISC programs are longer and require more memory to get stored but require fewer transistors due to less complex command.
25. 1. Focus on software Focus on hardware
26. 3. Transistors are used for more registers Transistors are used for storing complex
27. 4. Fixed sized instructions Variable sized instructions
28. 5. Can perform only Register to Register Can perform REG to REG or REG to Arithmetic operations MEM or MEM to MEM
29. 6. Requires more number of registers Requires less number of registers
30. 7. Code size is large Code size is small
31. 8. An instruction executed in a single Instruction takes more than one clock clock cycle cycle
008.qa_output: RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) instructions finish in a single clock cycle because each instruction is simple and designed for fast execution.  Although a RISC program is longer (requiring more individual instructions for a complex task), each of those simple instructions executes very quickly.  This contrasts with CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer), where complex instructions can take multiple clock cycles.  The efficiency in RISC comes from minimizing cycles per instruction, even if it means increasing the number of instructions needed for a task.

012.student_question: Why would an instruction or an address ever need to be bigger than the actual data word size?
012.source_chunks:
1. Characteristic of RISC –
2. 1. Simpler instruction, hence simple instruction decoding.
3. 2. Instruction comes undersize of one word.
4. 3. Instruction takes a single clock cycle to get executed.
5. 4. More general-purpose registers.
6. 5. Simple Addressing Modes.
7. 6. Fewer Data types.
8. 7. A pipeline can be achieved.
9. Characteristic of CISC:
10. 1. Complex instruction, hence complex instruction decoding.
11. 2. Instructions are larger than one-word size.
12. 3. Instruction may take more than a single clock cycle to get executed.
13. 4. Less number of general-purpose registers as operations get performed in memory itself.
14. 5. Complex Addressing Modes.
15. 6. More Data types.
16. Example – Suppose we have to add two 8-bit numbers:
17. • CISC approach: There will be a single command or instruction for this like ADD which will perform the task.
18. • RISC approach: Here programmer will write the first load command to load data in registers then it will use a suitable operator and then it will store the result in the desired location.
19. So, add operation is divided into parts i.e. load, operate, store due to which RISC programs are longer and require more memory to get stored but require fewer transistors due to less complex command.
20. 1. Focus on software Focus on hardware
21. 3. Transistors are used for more registers Transistors are used for storing complex
22. 4. Fixed sized instructions Variable sized instructions
23. 5. Can perform only Register to Register Can perform REG to REG or REG to Arithmetic operations MEM or MEM to MEM
24. 6. Requires more number of registers Requires less number of registers
25. 7. Code size is large Code size is small
26. 8. An instruction executed in a single Instruction takes more than one clock clock cycle cycle
27. Instruction and Instruction Types
28. Beyond the basic RISC/CISC characterization, we can classify computers by several characteristics of their instruction sets. The instruction set of the computer defines the interface between software modules and the underlying hardware; the instructions define what the hardware will do under certain circumstances. Instructions can have a variety of characteristics, including:
29. o fixed versus variable length;
30. o addressing modes;
31. o numbers of operands;
32. o types of operations supported.
33. We often characterize architectures by their word length: 4-bit, 8-bit, 16- bit, 32-bit, and so on. In some cases, the length of a data word, an instruction, and an address are the same. Particularly for computers designed to operate on smaller words, instructions and addresses may be longer than the basic data word.
34. Students should check out Little-endian vs. big-endian
35. We can also characterize processors by their instruction execution, a separate concern from the instruction set. A single-issue processor executes one instruction at a time. Although it may have several instructions at different stages of execution, only one can be at any particular stage of execution. Several other types of processors allow multiple-issue instruction. A superscalar processor uses specialized logic to identify at run time instructions that can be executed simultaneously.
36. The set of registers available for use by programs is called the programming model, also known as the programmer model. (The CPU has many other registers that are used for internal operations and are unavailable to programmers.)
012.qa_output:
In some computer systems, particularly those designed for smaller data words (e.g., 4-bit or 8-bit data), instructions and memory addresses may be longer than the basic data word size. This allows for more complex instructions and a wider range of memory locations to be addressed.

*   **Instruction Length:** Instructions can be larger than one-word size in CISC architectures, meaning a single instruction might span multiple data word units. This is to accommodate the complexity of operations that a single CISC instruction can perform, such as loading, operating, and storing data.
*   **Address Length:** The length of an address determines how many unique memory locations can be referenced. A longer address allows the CPU to access a larger amount of memory.

013.student_question: What is the difference between a single-issue processor and a superscalar processor? Do they both run multiple things at once?
013.source_chunks:
1. We often characterize architectures by their word length: 4-bit, 8-bit, 16- bit, 32-bit, and so on. In some cases, the length of a data word, an instruction, and an address are the same. Particularly for computers designed to operate on smaller words, instructions and addresses may be longer than the basic data word.
2. Students should check out Little-endian vs. big-endian
3. We can also characterize processors by their instruction execution, a separate concern from the instruction set. A single-issue processor executes one instruction at a time. Although it may have several instructions at different stages of execution, only one can be at any particular stage of execution. Several other types of processors allow multiple-issue instruction. A superscalar processor uses specialized logic to identify at run time instructions that can be executed simultaneously.
4. The set of registers available for use by programs is called the programming model, also known as the programmer model. (The CPU has many other registers that are used for internal operations and are unavailable to programmers.)
013.qa_output:
A single-issue processor executes one instruction at a time. Even if multiple instructions are in different stages of execution, only one instruction can be in any given stage.

A superscalar processor, conversely, uses specialized logic to identify and execute instructions simultaneously. This allows for multiple instructions to be in the same stage of execution at once.

Single-issue processors do not run multiple things at once. Superscalar processors do, by executing multiple instructions concurrently.

014.student_question: What is a "programmer model," and why can't we access the other internal registers in the CPU?
014.source_chunks:
1. We often characterize architectures by their word length: 4-bit, 8-bit, 16- bit, 32-bit, and so on. In some cases, the length of a data word, an instruction, and an address are the same. Particularly for computers designed to operate on smaller words, instructions and addresses may be longer than the basic data word.
2. Students should check out Little-endian vs. big-endian
3. We can also characterize processors by their instruction execution, a separate concern from the instruction set. A single-issue processor executes one instruction at a time. Although it may have several instructions at different stages of execution, only one can be at any particular stage of execution. Several other types of processors allow multiple-issue instruction. A superscalar processor uses specialized logic to identify at run time instructions that can be executed simultaneously.
4. The set of registers available for use by programs is called the programming model, also known as the programmer model. (The CPU has many other registers that are used for internal operations and are unavailable to programmers.)
014.qa_output: The programming model, also called the programmer model, is the set of registers available for use by programs. The CPU has other registers for internal operations that are not accessible to programmers.

015.student_question: In that RTN notation R3 <- [R1] + [R2], what do the brackets actually mean? Why aren't they around R3?
015.source_chunks:
1. INSTRUCTION SEQUENCING
2. Four types of operations
3. 1. Data transfer between memory and processor registers.
4. 2. Arithmetic & logic operations on data
5. 3. Program sequencing & control
6. 4. I/O transfers.
7. 1)Register transfer notations (RTN)
8. • Right hand side of RTN-denotes a value.
9. • Left hand side of RTN-name of a location.
10. • Adding contents of R1, R2 & place sum in R3.
11. 3)Basic instruction types-(4 types)
12. • Three address instructions–
13. • Two address instructions-
14. Equivalent to : B <–[A] + [B]
15. • One address instruction –
16. Add contents of A to accumulator & store sum back to accumulator.
17. • Zero address instructions
18. Instruction store operands in a structure called push down stack.
19. Instruction execution & straight line sequencing
20. • The processor control circuits use information in PC to fetch & execute instructions one at a time in order of increasing address. This is called straight line sequencing.
21. • Executing an instruction-2 phase procedures.
22. • 1st phase–“instruction fetch”-instruction is fetched from memory location whose address is in PC. This instruction is placed in instruction register in processor
23. • 2nd phase-“instruction execute”-instruction in IR is examined to determine which operation to be performed.
24. To be continued
015.qa_output:
In Register Transfer Notation (RTN), the brackets around R1 and R2, i.e., [R1] and [R2], signify that you are referring to the **contents** of those registers. The right-hand side of an RTN expression denotes a value. Therefore, [R1] + [R2] means the sum of the values stored in R1 and R2.

The left-hand side of an RTN expression, such as R3, names a location. Since R3 is on the left, it represents the register where the result of the operation (the sum) will be stored. Brackets are not used around R3 because it is the destination location, not a value being read for computation.

016.student_question: For a zero-address instruction, if there are no addresses in the command, how does the computer know what numbers it's supposed to add?
016.source_chunks:
1. Typically, a memory module will consist of N words of equal length.
2. Each word is assigned a unique numerical address (0, 1, N-1).
3. A word of data can be read from or written into the memory.
4. The nature of the operation is indicated by read and write control signals.
5. The location for the operation is specified by an address.
6. INSTRUCTION SEQUENCING
7. Four types of operations
8. 1. Data transfer between memory and processor registers.
9. 2. Arithmetic & logic operations on data
10. 3. Program sequencing & control
11. 4. I/O transfers.
12. 1)Register transfer notations (RTN)
13. • Right hand side of RTN-denotes a value.
14. • Left hand side of RTN-name of a location.
15. • Adding contents of R1, R2 & place sum in R3.
16. 3)Basic instruction types-(4 types)
17. • Three address instructions–
18. • Two address instructions-
19. Equivalent to : B <–[A] + [B]
20. • One address instruction –
21. Add contents of A to accumulator & store sum back to accumulator.
22. • Zero address instructions
23. Instruction store operands in a structure called push down stack.
24. Instruction execution & straight line sequencing
25. • The processor control circuits use information in PC to fetch & execute instructions one at a time in order of increasing address. This is called straight line sequencing.
26. • Executing an instruction-2 phase procedures.
27. • 1st phase–“instruction fetch”-instruction is fetched from memory location whose address is in PC. This instruction is placed in instruction register in processor
28. • 2nd phase-“instruction execute”-instruction in IR is examined to determine which operation to be performed.
29. To be continued
016.qa_output: Zero address instructions store operands in a structure called a push down stack. The operands are implicitly accessed from the top of this stack, and the result is also placed back onto the stack.
